key_insight: Gradient detonation-sprayed NiCr–Al coatings outperform homogeneous coatings under real thermal power plant conditions by maintaining a more protective oxide scale and slowing high-temperature oxidation.
engineering_relevance: These results support using composition-graded NiCr–Al coatings to extend the service life of hot-section industrial components exposed to severe oxidation and corrosive heat.
method: Detonation-sprayed gradient and homogeneous NiCr–Al coatings were fabricated.
High-temperature oxidation was tested under actual thermal power plant operating conditions.
Phase composition and SEM microstructure were analyzed to track oxide growth, elemental redistribution, and degradation.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that gradient coatings retained structural integrity better, formed more continuous Al2O3/Cr2O3 layers, and showed less oxidation kinetics than homogeneous coatings.
watch_item: Field performance may still depend on spray parameter control and coating-to-substrate adhesion under long-term thermal cycling.
paper_title: Investigation of Detonation-Sprayed NiCr–Al Coatings Tested under Real Operating Conditions of a Thermal Power Plant
